figures.caption: Fig. 8 Qualitative PI comparison on the CORPD dataset against DJS for a two-dimensional Gaussian k-space trajectory with and acceleration of 8. DJS introduces contrast artifacts in the lateral fat tissue, due to suboptimal coil sensitivity estimation. In contrast, these are not present in the reconstructions of our models. The inset shows a zoom on the image with corresponding absolute error. Our methods: Shearlet (SH), fully-learned filters (FLF) and fully-learned filters and time conditioning (FLF+TC).

Fig. 9 Qualitative PI comparison on the CORPDFS dataset against the VN and TV. First row: 4-fold Cartesian k-space trajectory with 8% available ACLs and an acceleration of 4. ACLs. Second row: 4-fold Cartesian k-space trajectory with 4% ACLs and an acceleration of 4. The inset shows a zoom on the image with corresponding absolute error. Our methods: Fully-learned filters (FLF) and fully-learned filters and time conditioning (FLF+TC).

Table 3 Quantitative results for PI experiments on the contrast out-of-distribution dataset (CORPDFS). The rows alternate between PSNR, SSIM and NMSE. The NMSE is scaled by 102. All metrics are shown as mean ± unit standard deviation. Bold typeface indicates the best method. Our methods: Shearlet (SH), fully-learned filters (FLF) and fully-learned filters and time conditioning (FLF+TC). Comparison methods: Zero filled (ZF), total variation (TV) [6], end-to-end variational network (VN) [77] and Deep-JSense (DJS) [25].

Fig. 12 Qualitative comparison of pixel-wise variance maps for the shearlet model, model with fullylearned filters and the model with fully-learned filters and time conditioning for a Cartesian k-space trajectory with 8% available ACLs and increasing acceleration factors between three and five. The left column shows the RSS reference, and the zero-filled reconstruction. In the right columns, the rows alternate between the Monte Carlo estimates of the approximate posterior expectation reconstructions for the different methods and their corresponding pixel-wise variance maps. Our methods: Shearlet (SH), fully-learned filters (FLF) and fully-learned filters and time conditioning (FLF+TC).

Table 2 Quantitative PI reconstruction results on the in-distribution knee dataset with proton density contrast. The rows alternate between PSNR, SSIM and NMSE. The NMSE is scaled by 102. All metrics are shown as mean ± unit standard deviation. Bold typeface indicates the best method. Our methods: Shearlet (SH), fully-learned filters (FLF) and fully-learned filters and time conditioning (FLF+TC). Comparison methods: Zero filled (ZF), total variation (TV) [6], end-to-end variational network (VN) [77] and Deep-JSense (DJS) [25].

Table 7 Influence of different sharing strategies of the Gaussian mixture factors on the CORPD dataset. The rows alternate between PSNR, SSIM and NMSE. The NMSE is scaled by 102. All metrics are shown as mean ± unit standard deviation. Bold typeface indicates the best method.

Table 6 Quantitative PI reconstruction results for different shearlet scalings j on the CORPD dataset. The rows alternate between PSNR, SSIM and NMSE. The NMSE is scaled by 102. All metrics are shown as mean ± unit standard deviation. Bold typeface indicates the best method. Comparison method: Zero filled (ZF).

Fig. 4 Time conditioning according to (24) for the model with fully-learned (5× 5) filters and time conditioning in the interval [0, 1] in black (left). The inset shows a detailed view of the interval [0, 0.25], highlighting the time conditioning for ψ1 and ψ2. The right plot shows the potentials (− logψ1 top, − logψ2 bottom) at diffusion times √ 2t = 0 , 0.05 , 0.2 . The corresponding values of the time conditioning are marked in the inset plot with × in the same colors.

Fig. 6 Qualitative PI comparison on the CORPD dataset against the VN in the failure case. First row: 4-fold Cartesian k-space trajectory with 4% available ACLs and an acceleration of 4. Second row: Radial k-space trajectory with an acceleration of 11. Here the VN introduces structures that are not existent in the RSS reference. The inset shows a zoom on the image with corresponding absolute error. Our methods: Shearlet (SH), fully-learned filters (FLF) and fully-learned filters and time conditioning (FLF+TC).

Fig. 11 Qualitative PI comparison on the brain T2 dataset against TV. First row: 4-fold Cartesian k-space trajectory with 8% available ACLs and an acceleration of 4. Second row: Same as row one with 4% available ACLs. Third row: Same as row one with rotated phase-encoding direction. Fourth row: 2D Gaussian k-space trajectory with an acceleration of 8. Fifth row: Radial k-space trajectory with an acceleration of 11. The inset shows a zoom on the image with corresponding absolute error. Our methods: Shearlet (SH), fully-learned filters (FLF) and fully-learned filters and time conditioning (FLF+TC).

Fig. 5 Time conditioning according to (18) for the convolutional model with fully-learned (5× 5) filters in the interval [0, 1] in black (left). The inset shows a detailed view of the interval [0, 0.25], highlighting the time conditioning for ψ15 and ψ2. The right plot shows the potentials (− logψ15 top, − logψ2 bottom) at diffusion times √ 2t = 0 , 0.05 , 0.2 . The corresponding values of the time conditioning are marked in the inset plot with × in the same colors.

Fig. 3 The learned model with Gaussian mixture factors, fully-learned zero-mean filters of size (5 × 5) and learned time conditioning. The variances of the Gaussian mixture factors evolve with diffusion time according to (24). The potentials are shown for different diffusion times √ 2t = 0 , 0.025 , 0.05 , 0.1 , 0.2 .
Fig. 13 RSS nullspace residuals for Cartesian k-space trajectories. In each block, the measured data has 8% available ACLs (left) and 4% available ACLs (right).

Fig. 10 Qualitative PI comparison on the brain T1 dataset against TV. First row: 4-fold Cartesian k-space trajectory with 8% available ACLs and an acceleration of 4. Second row: Same as row one with 4% available ACLs. Third row: Same as row one with rotated phase-encoding direction. Fourth row: 2D Gaussian k-space trajectory with an acceleration of 8. Fifth row: Radial k-space trajectory with an acceleration of 11. The inset shows a zoom on the image with corresponding absolute error. Our methods: Shearlet (SH), fully-learned filters (FLF) and fully-learned filters and time conditioning (FLF+TC).

Fig. 7 Qualitative PI comparison on the CORPD dataset against TV. First row: 4-fold Cartesian k-space trajectory with 8% available ACLs and an acceleration of 4. Second row: Same as row one with 4% available ACLs. Third row: Same as row one with rotated phase-encoding direction. Fourth row: 2D Gaussian k-space trajectory with an acceleration of 8. Fifth row: Radial k-space trajectory with an acceleration of 11. The inset shows a zoom on the image with corresponding absolute error. Our methods: Shearlet (SH), fully-learned filters (FLF) and fully-learned filters and time conditioning (FLF+TC).

Fig. 1 The learned shearlet model with Gaussian mixture factors and two shearlet scales. The first block shows the spectra of the learned shearlet filters. In the second block, the first row shows the vertical cone and the second row the horizontal cone of the shearlet system. The first five entries correspond to the shearing for the first scale and the second five for the second scale. The potentials and the negative logarithm of the empirical response marginals are shown for different diffusion times√ 2t = 0 , 0.025 , 0.05 , 0.1 , 0.2 .

Fig. 2 The learned model with Gaussian mixture factors and fully-learned zero-mean filters of size (5× 5). The variances of the Gaussian mixture factors evolve with diffusion time according to (18). The potentials are shown for different diffusion times √ 2t = 0 , 0.025 , 0.05 , 0.1 , 0.2 .
